text.title: Methodology as JSON Feed Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Methodology as JSON Feed application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every JSON response MUST carry a `schema_version` field. Responses without it are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without a schema version, agents cannot detect breaking changes; version-pinning is the only safe upgrade path.

rule.statement: Top-level keys (slug, version, summary, content, citation) MUST NOT change names across minor versions. Renames trigger a major version bump.
rule.rationale: Key renames break every adapter silently; the stability contract is the entire point of a programmatic feed.

rule.statement: Each item MUST include a `citation` field in `[faion:<slug>@<version>]` shape. Items without citation are rejected.
rule.rationale: Pre-baking the citation removes the responsibility from the agent and the human; attribution becomes automatic.

rule.statement: List responses MUST include `page`, `total`, `next_cursor`. Unbounded lists are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without pagination, large content responses time out or truncate silently; the explicit contract makes the boundary visible.

rule.statement: Fields MUST be typed (string, integer, boolean, array, object). Free-form prose blobs are rejected outside the `content` text field.
rule.rationale: Prose blobs in metadata fields force agents to LLM-parse what should be parsed by code; typed fields are the contract surface.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
